chunks.text: Becky is the Deputy Secretary, for Environment. I also have a number of my MFE officials with me if required this morning. The government is focused on delivering on its climate strategy to cut emissions and adapt to a changing climate while driving economic growth. Our first years in government laid the foundations of the plans, like the Second Emissions Reduction Plan and National Adaptation Framework. This year, we are continuing to deliver those plans. We have a clear work programme, and we're making good progress against

milestones. Our latest projections show that we will likely meet our first emissions budget, and we are on track to achieve the second. We are also on track to achieve our net zero target by 2050 and may achieve it as early as 2044. Over four years, we've committed $400 million to accelerate the development and commercialization of agricultural tools and technologies to reduce agricultural emissions. We are also making significant progress on renewables and electrification, progressing Electrify New Zealand to drive investment in renewable electricity by removing barriers

to consenting. Projects currently before the expert panel, already approved through the fast-track approvals process, could add around an additional 1,700 megawatt hours of new generation. This represents close to an increase of 20% of New Zealand's current capacity. We're also investing just over $50 million in zero interest loans to help double the number of public EV chargers with the support of the private sector with co-investment.

Recently in the budget, you would have seen the announcements around the gas transition loan guaranteeing scheme, which will make up to $1.2 billion of bank loans available to businesses to eliminate or reduce their dependency on reticulated gas. We're also investing, in the budget, $30 million in the Solar on Schools program, which will provide up to 500 schools the ability to install solar panels and batteries.

This aligns with other work around EDB regulation, making sure that consumers get fair market export tariffs and remove barriers for rooftop solar, which helps our broader emissions targets. We're also driving work through the National Adaptation Framework to build an enduring system to prepare for the impact of climate change. Just over $11 or $10.5 million has been invested in the Maori Climate Platform to support climate resilient projects.

This work is underway on the ground. Includes Marae-based solar, flood mitigation, and battery and water storage. We're developing New Zealand's first flood map, continuing investment in Budget 26 will ensure that communities have trusted, consistent picture of flood hazard information and better data to make more informed decisions when extreme weather is becoming more frequent and severe. And shortly, we'll be introducing legislation requiring councils to prepare adaptation plans for high priority areas. This works in hand with our new planning

system. Budget 26 invests in simpler, faster, and more consistent planning system that makes it easier to get things done. The new system will also include goals to safeguard communities from the risks of natural hazards and improve our understanding as an important precursor to managing natural hazard risks. Lastly, Madam Chair, our work ahead focuses on our climate goals while supporting our community, environment, and economy to thrive.

And with that, I'll welcome questions from the committee. Thank you very much, Minister, for that overview. We will start with Deborah Russell. Thank you. Minister, I guess there's a whole lot of stuff that I want to talk about, but let's... I know that Chloe has stuff that she wants to talk about as well.

I guess my major concern is around the emissions trading scheme and the extent to which work needs to go on to ensure that the scheme continues to function effectively and that the auctions work as needed. We know that it's, under current settings, the ETS looks like it will run out, as it were, in the 2030s.

This morning, the unit price is sitting at about $53 a ton, so well below what we need. That's from a secondary market, and we actually need the price to be quite a bit higher than that for the auctions to start clearing. So could you let us know what work you are doing or intend to do to ensure that the emissions trading scheme does actually start to function as a tool for driving down emissions?

Yeah, thanks for the question. Obviously, as minister, I won't comment on the ETS market prices. However, I will note that the ETS market remains cautious, with high interest in climate policy that the government releases. As you've noted, the price is currently sitting in around above the low 50s, and that has been the case for most of May and June. In regards to the key aspect of intervention by government, it is to ensure, and our climate strategy outlines this, that we want a credible ETS market in order to do the heavy lifting around emissions

reduction. The point that you're noting is, is that the price point at where it's at at the market at the moment versus the point of tension of what that price needs to be to actually see the emissions reductions coming down. There is two points in that context. The government's policy has been one of applying certainty. We're currently out for consultation in regards to the settings of the ETS market, and we'll be taking feedback in regards to that.

But it's also fair, as you've noted, that actually, when you look at carbon prices internationally, and particularly the UK and EU prices, have seen significant reductions in that price point in the last six to nine months. So we are seeing a degree of volatility in an international sense around carbon pricing as well, and for multiple reasons in regards to that Oh.

Chloe just had a supplementary on that- Oh, I wonder if I might have a couple of questions... if you want me to carry on. Yeah, go. Yeah. No, then we'll hand over to you. And I'm not going to take too much time. It's just a supplement ETS, but then, yeah. That's okay. Okay. Yeah, we'll come to you on the ETS questions as well- Yeah, before we go to primary... Chloe, but if Deborah can kind of- Sure, no worries... take the original question line. What was I going to ask? Oh. Yeah, we use the word volatility, but I wonder if the better word is skepticism about ETS markets

worldwide, to the extent that pricing is not going to drive the changes we need, and we may need to regulate in order to drive emissions down. The wording I used deliberately was that the market remains cautious, and I think that would be a fair assessment in terms of where- Okay... the market is in the context of our broader climate policy. What's interesting is that the price of the market has returned to levels comparable to where they were prior to the CCRRA amendments announced in November of last year. And the other factor in play in the New Zealand market is the

forestry supply and demand. And there is a degree of uncertainty in regards to that aspect, which does have an impact in regards to future prices. That's a lovely segue, because that's exactly where I was going to go next, was forestry. So we've had quite a bit of commentary, especially from the Parliamentary Commissioner from the Environment, but also from other people, that exotic forestry in the ETS is creating problems. And part of the reason is as that carbon price goes, sitting around about 53, that incentivizes more planting of exotic forestry.

But also we just don't quite know how much exotic forestry is out there that could come into the ETS. What work are you doing around the role of exotic forestry in the ETS? So one of the announcements and policy changes that the government has made in this term is in regards to the introduction of, in effect, a restriction of the amount of exotic forestry that is able to be- Sure... go into the ETS. And so those announcements were made last year, in effect providing a cap depending on the land use classification aspect. Before that came into place, there was, in effect, no

cap or restriction on the amount of exotic forestry that was able to go into the ETS. That has provided, in effect, a cap, and we'll be monitoring that aspect as it plays through. Sam, I don't know if there's any other context that you wanted to provide. No. But general point is that there are trade-offs that any policy decision makers have to weigh up when considering the role of forestry and volume of forestry within the ETS.

And forestry can help to reduce the cost of the transition, but as others have pointed to, it comes with some trade-offs- Yeah... that, yeah, are material, will need to be considered carefully. So one last question before I just- Yes, go for it... desist.

So again, it's around forestry. So my understanding is that we just don't know how much exotic forestry is out there that may come into the ETS, and that's one of the problems that's creating uncertainty. And of course, it flows into issues around the register and all sorts of things like that. Absolutely. So I wonder if you could just comment on that as to what we know about end of use that may come into the ETS because of exotic forestry, and as it were, a stocktake of exotic forestry that's sitting out there that might into the ETS.

Yeah. It's absolutely one of the areas that, as I've highlighted, is the aspect that does bring volatility to the market because of the awareness and the understanding of the demand and supply. Becky will be able to probably give a little bit more context on that. Yeah. There's not a lot of concern that there might be sort of existing exotic forests-... that are not in the ETS but might come in.

I think maybe, do you mean that there's uncertainty around how much planting there might be in the future, and when that would come in? Yeah. Yeah. Yeah, that's true. The future projections for how much planting might take place are always a bit uncertain, just I guess with anything in the future. We have things like surveys of foresters' intentions that give us a sense of how much to project, and that kind of thing.

I think that's what you're talking about. Yeah, that's what Yeah. There is always uncertainty. Since it is a significant part of the emissions trading team, it has a significant impact because of that uncertainty, but it's not sort of inherently more uncertain than anything else in the climate change area. Okay. All right. Thank you. One of the key aspects around forestry particularly is that we do acknowledge that it is a long-term investment, and the horizon for that investment is multi-decade. And therefore, the

importance of having certainty of a policy position in regards to that, because they are, in effect, private property rights for individuals in the context of the units that are held, and decisions and implications around policy does have an implication on value of existing private property rights. So that component is equally important. But we are looking to see how do we get better information and data on the intentions, but acknowledging forecasts are, in many aspects, a degree of art, not science, because of the reality of

AR forecasting. Thank you. Thank you. Look, we'll stay on ETS and we'll come to Chloe. Yeah, I just had three key questions in the space of the ETS. Minister, just to reflect on your answers there, you've obviously alluded to the fact that this government's kind of major tool in emissions mitigation is the ETS. You've said that it needs certainty and that you want a credible market.

But here, I'm quoting from the Climate Change Commission's annual advice, in which it said, quote, "A significant drop in NZU prices followed government policy announcements on the 4th of November 2025. Market participants have reported that the perception of continued weakening of climate policy in general is contributing to low market sentiment." To that effect, it is apparently abundantly clear to all market players and to the expert independent advisors to the government that the reason that the ETS, which you say you want to be credible, is failing, is as a

result of your government policy. What do you say about that? Yeah, well, I completely disagree with that. The quote that you're making is referenced in a point in time of, if I heard you rightly, November of last year. And I made this in my opening statements before, that the precision of where the market is now at is, is it has returned to levels comparable to those prior to the amendment points. They're sitting in the low 50s.

At the point in which that advice was provided, the market was trading in the upper 30s, and the reality of the market is, is it has taken a period of time to digest the reality of the policy change. I said at the time that the policy change does not at all impact the fundamentals of the market.

However, the market has taken time, but is now trading back. So the reference of the quote is in a point of time of which is not the reality of where we are today. So why then have the vast majority of emissions trading scheme auctions failed under your tenure? If it's that the market players are wrong and the independent expert advisors are wrong, why are you right?

So first and foremost, I don't agree with the premise that the market is failing. The market operates as any market would, and that is in order to meet demand and supply. And we're talking about the government market, not the secondary market, of which the prices are being quoted in the prior response. In effect, and this links into the aspect around the conversation on forestry is, is that the demand for units that government auction is not sufficient to meet the price and the strike price in which we're offering those.

And the market is able to obtain the units through the secondary market and doesn't rely on the auctions. The fact that the auctions are not clearing is not a indication that there is a market failure. Actually, it is an indication that the market is working as it should do, i.e. demand meets supply, and that is what's happening.

We remain of the view, though, that we do want the ETS to do the heavy lifting around emissions reduction. We want there to be, and we want the ETS to be a credible market. About $8 billion of trades go through that market every year. It is a significant financial market and goes back to the property rights. And the role of government policy does have quite a significant impact on that market, unlike, say, equity markets or other financial markets that are in play. So we take that very seriously.

And likewise, the economics of where the ETS price sits also has quite significant economic implications on broader, say, the forestry sector, for example, as well. So it's simply acknowledging that from policy point of view, we want to be very clear and concise around what we will do and not do.

Stability of policy positions, no surprises, and we do actively seek feedback from the market and market participants around their ideas of how we could strengthen the ETS. It is government's position clearly in our strategy that we want a credible ETS market, and that's representative of the actions government takes.

I think market participants have been abundantly clear. Are you aware of the fact that under your current plans, in order to meet Emissions Budget 3, you are requiring 46% of planned emissions reductions to come through forestry? So in regards to Emissions Budget 3, which is for the period 2031 to 2035, we're currently tracking as of the latest projections at 3.6% below that target, and that target is that of 2035. I'm confident that while we still have around 8.7 million tons of CO2 to make up between now and 2035,

2035 is not tomorrow. We have time, and we have a comprehensive program of actions underway. Any future point forecast, there is always challenge, either under or over. What I am happy, though, is, is that EB1, for the period ending 2025, and EB2, for the period ending 2030, both of those, based on the current trajectory of this government's climate policy, are on track to meet those targets and actually exceed them based on their forecasts at the moment. And when we're talking plus or minus 3% under and over budget, five or seven years or nine years from out,

I think in the scheme of challenge and opportunity, we're doing okay. We have adaptive management procedures in place where ministers meet on a regular basis to discuss if we're not tracking on the target, what do we need to adapt to that. And I lead those conversations, and we had one in the last few months actually, where ministers discussed plans to ensure that we remain on track for our target.

That wasn't my question, Minister. My question was about whether you're aware- It's okay... that under current plans to meet Emissions Budget 3, you are relying for at least 46% of those emissions reductions to come from forestry. Are you aware of that? Well, I wrote the plan, so with respect, I am aware of the policy. So, the emissions reduction- That's good to know. So sorry. I'm just aware that we have such limited time.

Just enable the minister to- But we have such limited time... just finish, Chloe, and then we will move. I'd really appreciate a back and forth so we can actually have some answers. Yeah. I just answered the- Yeah, cool. Great. So thank you for that... Yeah, I wrote the plan. So to that effect Have you got a different question? No, it's related to particularly the forestry point. Okay. Yeah. One more question, Chloe, and then we'll move on. Minister There's lots of areas here to- Have you engaged at all with the conservation minister or other

environmental ministers who are currently proposing serious changes to the Conservation Act, which would enable the sell-off of our conservation estate, which would therefore have an effect on carbon sequestration in this country, a huge swath of this country, and the impact that that will have on the ability to sequester carbon, and therefore meet these emissions budgets? I regularly meet with my ministerial colleagues, including the Minister for Conservation, to talk about my portfolio areas and their portfolios.

Have you engaged on the Conservation Act? They are representative as part of the broader ministerial group that we meet regularly around climate targets. My interaction with those ministers in that regard is under those aspects of governance. Have you raised any risks about the ability for us to sequester carbon as a result of the proposed changes under the Conservation Act to sell off and enable economic development on our conservation estate?

I'm comfortable that the changes proposed by the Minister of Conservation in regards to changes around the Conservation Act do not provide the material risk that you are implying in regards to our broader targets. Look, thank you. And we will obviously have the conservation minister in later today, that will also be able to address some of those questions.

This is the prime minister you should be asking. We'll come to Grant McCallum for a question. Thank you, Madam Chair. Morning, Minister. Last week, I attended Fieldays, and we made an announcement around the AgriZero Early Adopter Accelerator program. You want to just sort of walk us through the $51 million we announced and how you see that's going to help with emissions reduction tools on farm?

Mm. Good question. Yeah. Thanks, Grant, and I thought the conversations that I had at Fieldays in regards to the progress that we're making, in that regard, were very positive. Mm. I think it is important to highlight, and often, the success of AgriZero and the $400 million and the additional funding that was announced at budget is one of the success stories of how private sector and government and NGO are working together around solving probably which is one of the most complex- Mm... climate challenges, which is around methane

reduction. AgriZero have around 17 different work streams currently underway. A number of those work streams actually will have tools that are going to be available in market by the end of this year. And that is very positive. They include inhibitors, probiotics, vaccines.

There are also work, as you'll be aware, on the aspect around oxidation ponds, as well, and reduction of emissions in that area, pasture, and also the genetics work that's underway- Yeah... both for beef cows and sheep in that context. So look, I think that it is positive, and it's interesting also that particularly Ireland, which is a pastoral-based nation, which also is dealing with the same grappling issues around that, how New Zealand and Ireland are working collaboratively around the science space around methane reduction because we're both

pastoral-based farming systems. The challenge is also one for us around methane reduction, but the biggest challenge is when you think about who has the largest dairy herd in the world, India- Sure... and other jurisdictions like that, how New Zealand can actually play to our strengths as an R&D and innovator- Sure... and share that technology with other countries.

Mm. It is quite exciting, I think, around the opportunity of New Zealand playing a leadership role around- Sure... technology that not only increases productivity and economic- Sure... gain for farmers, which is first and foremost, but the secondary benefit of that is a reduction of emissions. And that's the sweet spot, really. So positive feedback, and as a dairy farmer, I'm sure you'll be, uh- Very aware, yeah... on that fondly.

Yeah. And just one fur- one sup on that. So why do you prefer that technological approach as opposed to what some would prefer to see, which is just a blanket reduction in animals? Well, because it's practical. Mm. And it's realistic. And it works. Uh, and it has engagement.

I think it'd be fair to say that no one wants to be in a position where industry are at war with government. Yeah. Right? And you're seeing that in other jurisdictions, and we had challenges in regards to that. We're now in a position where the government and industry are working with each other. There's no head in the sand that there isn't a challenge here-... that we need to deal with.

That's right. And I think under the leadership of AgriZero, with Rob Hewett- Yeah... who's a farmer, who is very much of the view that the challenge now, once we've got the tools, is how do you accelerate the uptake- That's right... of those interventions and how do you create a mechanism, which is the incentive, but also the tension for uptake. But we also have to be cautious because what we saw in Europe around some of the early- Bovine, yeah... fast adoption of these tools before they were fully proven and fully

tested, have had to retract, and then there's a loss of trust. And-... with any industry, I will back a farmer in the paddock to know their business better than anyone in this building, with the exception of a few of you at the table, because you are- Thank you. I appreciate that... both, but do you know what I mean? Yeah. We've got to just make sure that we're working with industry.

But the tension has been right, as is that, go back last year or the year before, even three years, there was a lot of talk about tools, but I think the confidence level that they would actually deliver emissions reduction... was not as high. Yeah, that's right. And I shared that concern, and I know many around the table would as well. I think we've worked hard to increase the transparency of what's happening, when it's going to happen, how much impact it will have around emissions reduction, and also equally, it's not just an emissions conversation, it's an economic growth,

productivity conversation. Because a more economic and productive agri-sector has more money to go around, which means that we've got more investment to do everything in regards to the broader challenges that we have, of which climate change is one. Thank you. And David just had a supplementary on that. And then Chloe does too. Yes.

So Minister, just following on from that, do you think that this approach is leading to the point of not needing methane pricing, and if not, why not? Well, the government has always been of the view that pricing in absence of practical tools and solutions for farmers to deploy to reduce emissions reduction is not going to be a feasible, nor enduring mechanism to reduce emissions.

It is sort of at one degree common sense that in order to reduce emissions, you need to have the means and mechanism to do so without, in effect, which is what some European countries are doing, is culling their herds and reducing their productivity and their production. Of which we know, if New Zealand did that, it would simply be exported to another jurisdiction. Which New Zealand farmers are some of the most carbon efficient pastoral farmers in the world, and so therefore, that doesn't help anyone.

So we very much do invest in science, technology and innovation, the tools and techniques that allow us to increase productivity and emissions, and work with the farming community to deploy and increase the uptake of those as and when they are credible and they are appropriate and ready to go to market. Acknowledging consumers are very sensitive to new technology across the board, not just in this area.

So, we're doing that, and to pair that, we're working with colleagues such as Ireland, such as Australia, so that we've got multiple countries working together on this type of area so that we're not out of step. Again, I see this as probably one of the most fundamental and game-changing areas of emissions reduction for climate and productivity increase actually for- That's right... agriculture at a global level.

Oh, yeah, Agri Zero. And I spoke about that in COP when I had a panel on sustainable farming. Agri Zero, yeah. And actually, again, we often probably underestimate how advanced New Zealand is in the space. And while we wished we'd have those tools three years ago, we are well advanced of any other jurisdiction in the world around increasing productivity on farm and getting the by-product of that, which is emissions reduction. So, let's see how we go in the next 12 to 18 months.

Thank you. We'll just take another couple of supplementaries on agricultural emissions. Chloe, and then come to Deborah. Minister, can I just confirm that the fund, that that announcement at Fieldays came from, was the AgTech fund, which was funded by the former government and then reannounced by your government in 2024, after your government had cut approximately $50 million from it?

No, I can't confirm your points that you're making in the second part of your- I've got an OIA from Treasury that says that... statement there. But the funding announcement was made by Minister Maclay, so it's best directed to him in regards to that technicality. And which fund does it come from, sorry? Which fund? I said the announcement was made by Minister Maclay.

Yeah, but you just spent quite a long time talking about it. Which fund is it? I talked about it because in the context of emissions reduction, any investment across any portfolio is a... Let's just remember the way that climate change works under the government. I have accountability on behalf of the Prime Minister to ensure that New Zealand meets its targets. The relevant ministers who have portfolio responsibility, agriculture, energy, transport, have responsibility to ensure that their portfolio delivers the actions required in order to reduce emissions in

their portfolio areas. It is a matrix, and that is why my role is to ensure that I work with my colleague ministers, to deliver that. I understand. There's nothing wrong with another minister using their budget to fund an emissions reduction initiative. I understand all of those things, Minister, but you were just talking quite excitedly about the investment that's being made, and the point that I'm making is that you cut 50, well, your government cut $50 million from that investment that you were talking about as though it's an awesome thing.

It previously had been a larger fund under the former government. Yeah, when I wake up in the morning, I wake up thinking positively and- About cuts... I make no apology for being positive about the ambitions and optimism of the New Zealand primary sector. How have they managed it this time? And the role we're doing around research and development. We should all celebrate that. So ambitious, the bar of the Treasury. And I wish the opposition actually would celebrate that at some point, rather than being so negative.

And just sticking with agriculture emissions, Deborah, you've got a question. This one, I was going to make a similar point, that the commitment to Agri Zero is a cross-government one, that it started under the previous government and was continued by this government, and actually we're all committed to it because it's- Yeah, and look, I acknowledge that, Deborah. You're right. Thank you. If I could clarify the actual question.

I think the challenge with climate change is that we do- Yeah... policy flip-flop's not helpful. We want to make sure that we've got enduring aspect, and Agri Zero has been a real success. I think we've built on that and it's a great success. Well, let's leave policy flip-flops to one side, because we might push a bit. Look, I just want to, it's following on from a point that David made, that in the absence of technologies to actually reduce methane, it would not be particularly helpful to have a pricing mechanism in

there. But Minister, you've just said that in fact some of those technologies are coming on board quite soon, and I've done the same talking to you with Ruminant Biotech and the people doing the vaccines and this, that, and the other. So they're looking quite close. Does that imply that you will introduce a pricing mechanism once those technologies are available? No, it doesn't. Okay.

We've been very clear in regards to our policy intent that Uh, pricing, uh, is not, uh, helpful in the context of where, uh, we do not have, uh, the means for farmers to be able to reduce, uh, methane emission on farm. Our focus and priority is on ensuring they have the technology pipeline, the tools and development for New Zealand farmers to be able to reduce emissions. Uh, and actually what we're seeing already, uh, with, um, uh, initiatives such as Eco Pond.

We don't have pricing today, but it doesn't stop, uh, you know, dairy farmers in New Zealand and Fonterra and Synlait increasing the uptake of Eco Pond, which has a circa 9% reduction in regards to not only methane, but also, uh, nitrous oxide as well. So, um, look, farmers in New Zealand, as we know, have always been at the forefront of taking up, uh, innovation technology.

And the reason is, is that these innovation and technology, um, developments, first and foremost, are productivity enhancing developments. They increase production- That's right... they increase profitability. But like solar power, people are doing that because of the economics. Yes, there is a secondary benefit of climate, and that is great, but farmers are doing so because of the economic reality.

Uh, and you know, that's something that, as a government, we, uh, support and wish to see further of. With respect, Minister- Thank you. Just one last one on this. Yeah. It's quite important because, um, uh, when I've been meeting with Federated Farmers and Dairy NZ and so on, they've challenged me. They've said, "Hang on a second. Markets are driving the change in, um, our insustainability and around methane, and, um, uh, we'll do enough through market mechanisms to, to meet it." So I went and tested that with, um, some of our food processors, so people like Silver Fern Farms and

Fonterra and so on. And their response was that in actual fact, being clean and green, sustainable, uh, meeting our climate targets in these days only gets us to the table. It doesn't get us the market. Um, and in particular, we've got some very strong commitments, particularly in our EU trade agreements and so on, that we must continue to meet our environmental and our, and our, um- Climate... climate objectives. But at the same time, markets alone aren't going to do that in terms of getting change on farms.

So it does really rather imply that sooner or later we're going to need to have, as well as the carrots, um, of the higher prices that we get for sustainable products, we are going to need to have some sticks as well in the form of pricing mechanisms or regulation once we've got those tools available. So, you know, it does suggest that we may need to go on that route once those tools are available.

Have you got a question? Um- That's, I'm asking, that's the comment. That's sort of, it's responding back to what the minister said. As I set out, the priority of this government here and now is to ensure that farmers have practical tools to be able to reduce emissions, and investing in the, um, mitigation tools and the research and development pipeline that'll enable those tools to not only be a reality for New Zealand farmers, but be a reality for Irish farmers, for farmers in India, for farmers across the globe.

Because the opportunity of increased productivity and also emissions reduction, environment and economics on two sides of the same coin, is the, uh, real, uh, opportunity for New Zealand. And actually, you know, you're seeing that as well with other innovation tools that New Zealand are exporting to the world, uh, around farm productivity as well. So that's where our focus is here and now, uh, and that's, uh, where it will be.

Thank you. Look, we've got a... You've got a supplementary on this. Just a quick sup- Okay. Yes. Go for it, Cameron- Min-... and then we'll move on... Minister just raised quite a few countries there. Did you, is anybody looking at the, um, the South American, uh, examples like Uruguay and their approach to split gas and, um, warming from methane? Yeah. So New Zealand already does have a- Split gas... a split gas, uh, approach in regards to, uh, its, um, domestic, uh, target, uh, model.

Uh, and that is, uh, uh, deliberate. So that's the context between, uh, the broader 10%, um, uh, aspect and, uh, the other components being non-methane. You know, the, New Zealand interacts, uh, significantly across the board with a range of countries, uh, in this area. Our main interaction is with the Irish, it'd be fair to say, because of the similarities around the pastoral base farming system. Uh, and again, our focus really is, is around investment in R&D and innovation on productivity, uh, and, uh, emissions reduction.

That's where we- But Uruguay does have a pastoral base farming system as well. It's very similar to New Zealand. Has anyone looked at measuring that against what the Irish are doing, say, or other countries in the EU? Uh, yes. And, uh, there's been significant work underway. We're continually looking at, and working with constructively, with other farming nations in regards to process.

But what we're talking about here is less about what the targets are and the, and the process and the mechanism. What are the tools and actions we've got in the toolkit that we actually can take action and deliver outcomes? Mm-hmm. And that is where the focus is under the current government policy, first and foremost. We've reset our targets, um, in regards to a biogenic, uh, methane target, which we'll be aware of.

Um, and you know, we've interacted closely with, you know, UK and EU on our domestic policies and the policy implications that were raised previously. Uh, it is not of our view, or we obviously take our obligations under FTAs very seriously, uh, but, uh, we are, um, comfortable that the policy decisions that we're making, uh, do not have, uh, any, uh, significant implications in the context of our relationships, nor with our free trade agreements.
